findings.title: Secret detected: Detected a Generic API Key, potentially exposing access to various services and sensitive operations.
findings.description: Betterleaks detected a Detected a Generic API Key, potentially exposing access to various services and sensitive operations. in battle_test_candidates/go_target/main.go:31. Rule: generic-api-key. Entropy: 4.92
findings.tool_output: Rule: generic-api-key | Tags: [] | Entropy: 4.92 | Verified: false

findings.title: Potential G704: SSRF via taint analysis
findings.description:
SSRF via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the 'url' parameter in the HTTP request
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends GET request to /fetch?url=http://internal-service:8080/admin 2. Handler extracts 'url' parameter (line 155) 3. No allowlist validation (line 156 comment) 4. http.Get(targetURL) executed (line 157) 5. Response returned to attacker
attack_scenario.impact: attacker can probe internal network services (e.g., cloud metadata endpoints, internal APIs) and exfiltrate data via response
attack_scenario.explanation: The code directly uses user-supplied URL in http.Get without any allowlist or validation, enabling SSRF attacks against internal resources.

findings.title: Potential G701: SQL injection via taint analysis
findings.description:
SQL injection via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
findings.code_snippet:
71 | 70: 	query := "SELECT id, username, role FROM users WHERE username='" + username + "' AND password='" + password + "'"
71: 	row := db.QueryRow(query)
72:

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can supply username and password via HTTP POST form
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends POST to /login with username=' OR '1'='1' -- and password=anything 2. Handler concatenates into SQL query (line 70) 3. db.QueryRow executes (line 71) 4. Query returns first user (likely admin) 5. Attacker logs in as admin
attack_scenario.impact: attacker can bypass authentication and gain admin access, potentially leading to full database compromise
attack_scenario.explanation: String concatenation of user input into SQL query without parameterization allows classic SQL injection.

findings.title: Potential G703: Path traversal via taint analysis
findings.description:
Path traversal via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the 'filename' parameter in the HTTP request
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends GET to /read?filename=../../etc/passwd 2. Handler constructs filepath as './files/../../etc/passwd' (line 135) 3. os.ReadFile reads the file (line 136) 4. File content returned to attacker
attack_scenario.impact: attacker can read arbitrary files on the server, including configuration files, source code, and system files
attack_scenario.explanation: User-supplied filename is concatenated with a base path without sanitization, enabling path traversal.

findings.title: Potential G702: Command injection via taint analysis
findings.description:
Command injection via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
[Also reported by another step: Subprocess launched with variable
Gosec confidence: 90%]

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the 'cmd' parameter in the HTTP request
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends GET to /exec?cmd=ls;cat /etc/shadow 2. Handler constructs cmd string (line 113) 3. exec.Command('bash', '-c', cmd) executes (line 114) 4. Command output returned to attacker
attack_scenario.impact: attacker can execute arbitrary system commands with the privileges of the web server, leading to full server compromise
attack_scenario.explanation: User input is passed directly to bash -c without sanitization, allowing arbitrary command execution.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the 'input' parameter in the HTTP request
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends GET to /hash?input=<script>alert(1)</script> 2. Handler sets Content-Type to application/json (line 209) 3. fmt.Fprintf writes JSON with unsanitized input (line 210) 4. If browser interprets as HTML (e.g., via content sniffing), script executes
attack_scenario.impact: attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in victim's browser, potentially stealing cookies or performing actions on behalf of the user
attack_scenario.explanation: User input is reflected in JSON response without encoding; if Content-Type is not strictly enforced, XSS may occur.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — Content-Type is application/octet-stream, which forces binary download. No XSS vector exists because browsers will not render the response as HTML.]

attack_scenario.explanation: Content-Type is application/octet-stream, which forces binary download. No XSS vector exists because browsers will not render the response as HTML.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — Content-Type is text/plain, which prevents HTML rendering. No XSS vector exists.]

attack_scenario.explanation: Content-Type is text/plain, which prevents HTML rendering. No XSS vector exists.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the 'html' parameter in the HTTP request
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends GET to /render?html=<script>alert(1)</script> 2. Handler writes raw HTML to response (line 100) 3. Browser renders the HTML and executes the script
attack_scenario.impact: attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in victim's browser, leading to session hijacking, phishing, or defacement
attack_scenario.explanation: User-supplied HTML is written directly to response without sanitization or templating, enabling stored/reflected XSS.

findings.title: Potential G401: Use of weak cryptographic primitive
findings.description:
Use of weak cryptographic primitive
Gosec confidence: 90%
findings.code_snippet:
206 | 205: 	// VULNERABLE: MD5 is cryptographically broken
206: 	hash := md5.Sum([]byte(input))
207: 	hexHash := fmt.Sprintf("%x", hash)

attack_scenario.actor: attacker who can obtain MD5 hashes (e.g., via database leak or network sniffing)
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker obtains MD5 hash from response (line 210) 2. Attacker uses rainbow tables or brute force to reverse the hash 3. Attacker recovers the original input (e.g., password)
attack_scenario.impact: attacker can recover plaintext values from MD5 hashes, compromising user credentials or sensitive data
attack_scenario.explanation: MD5 is cryptographically broken and vulnerable to collision and preimage attacks; its use weakens security.

findings.title: Potential G114: Use of net/http serve function that has no support for setting timeouts
findings.description:
Use of net/http serve function that has no support for setting timeouts
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: remote attacker who can establish many slow connections to the server
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker opens many TCP connections to :8080 2. Server does not enforce timeouts (line 52) 3. Connections remain open indefinitely, exhausting server resources
attack_scenario.impact: denial of service: server becomes unresponsive to legitimate requests due to resource exhaustion
attack_scenario.explanation: http.ListenAndServe without timeouts allows slowloris-style attacks; however, severity is medium as it requires many connections.

findings.title: Potential G202: SQL string concatenation
findings.description:
SQL string concatenation
Gosec confidence: 90%
findings.code_snippet:
70 | 69: 	// VULNERABLE: string concatenation into SQL query
70: 	query := "SELECT id, username, role FROM users WHERE username='" + username + "' AND password='" + password + "'"
71: 	row := db.QueryRow(query)

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can supply username and password
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends POST to /login with malicious input 2. String concatenation builds SQL query (line 70) 3. db.QueryRow executes (line 71) 4. SQL injection succeeds
attack_scenario.impact: attacker can bypass authentication and extract/modify database contents
attack_scenario.explanation: Same as IRON-GOSEC-002; SQL string concatenation is vulnerable.

attack_scenario.explanation: Same as IRON-GOSEC-003; path traversal via variable.

findings.title: Potential Generic API key in config
findings.description: Regex pattern detected a potential Generic API key in config at main.go:31. This may be a hardcoded secret that automated scanners missed.
findings.code_snippet: 31 | const apiKey = "sk-abc123def456ghi789jkl012mno345pqr678stu"

attack_scenario.actor: Any attacker who can access the source code (e.g., via a public repository, insider threat, or compromised CI/CD pipeline). No special privileges required beyond read access to the codebase.
attack_scenario.path:
1. Attacker obtains the source file main.go (e.g., by cloning a public repo or exfiltrating from a compromised system).
2. Attacker reads line 31: `const apiKey = "sk-abc123def456ghi789jkl012mno345pqr678stu"`.
3. Attacker uses this API key to authenticate against the corresponding service (e.g., OpenAI, Stripe, or similar) and performs unauthorized actions.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker gains full access to the API service associated with the key, potentially leading to data exfiltration, service abuse, financial charges, or privilege escalation depending on the key's permissions. The key appears to be a valid format (e.g., 'sk-' prefix suggests a secret key for a service like OpenAI).
attack_scenario.explanation: The finding is a hardcoded API key in source code. The key is a literal string constant, not an environment variable or placeholder. The pattern 'sk-' is a common prefix for secret keys (e.g., OpenAI, Stripe). This is a real security vulnerability because anyone with access to the source can extract and misuse the key. The severity is HIGH as it can lead to significant impact.

findings.title: SBOM generated: 1 components detected
findings.description: Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) generated in CycloneDX format. 1 components inventoried for vulnerability tracking.

findings.title: Not a git repository — supply chain checks limited
findings.description: Supply chain security checks (SBOM provenance, SLSA) require a git repository. Without git history, we cannot verify commit signatures or build provenance.

findings.title: SBOM generation available via syft
findings.description: Run 'syft scan <target> -o cyclonedx-json' to generate a Software Bill of Materials. SBOMs are required for supply chain transparency (US Executive Order 14028).

findings.title: OpenSSF Scorecard not installed
findings.description: Install OpenSSF Scorecard (https://github.com/ossf/scorecard) to get automated security health checks for your dependencies and build pipeline.

findings.title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) in proxy handler
findings.description: The handleProxy function takes a user-controlled 'url' query parameter and passes it directly to http.Get() without any validation or allowlist. An attacker can make the server send requests to arbitrary internal or external hosts, potentially accessing internal services (e.g., cloud metadata endpoints, internal APIs) or performing port scanning.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement an allowlist of permitted target URLs or domains. Validate that the URL scheme is HTTPS and the host is in a predefined list. Alternatively, use a URL parser to reject private IP ranges and block access to internal networks.

findings.title: OS Command Injection in handleExec
findings.description: The handleExec function takes a 'cmd' query parameter from the URL and passes it directly to exec.Command with 'bash -c', allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary system commands. There is no authentication, input validation, or sanitization. This is a classic OS command injection vulnerability.
findings.fix_suggestion: Avoid using 'bash -c' with user input. If command execution is required, use a whitelist of allowed commands and validate input strictly. Consider using a safer alternative like exec.Command with fixed arguments, or implement proper input validation and authentication.

findings.title: Path Traversal in handleFiles
findings.description: The 'filename' parameter from the URL query string is concatenated into a file path without validation, allowing an attacker to read arbitrary files outside the intended './files/' directory by using path traversal sequences like '../'.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate and sanitize the filename: ensure it does not contain path traversal characters (e.g., '../'), or use a whitelist of allowed filenames. Alternatively, use filepath.Clean and check that the resolved path starts with the intended base directory.

findings.title: Reflected XSS in handleSearch
findings.description: User input from the 'query' query parameter is directly concatenated into an HTML string and written to the response without any escaping or sanitization. An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript by crafting a malicious query parameter, leading to reflected cross-site scripting (XSS).
findings.fix_suggestion: Use Go's html/template package to auto-escape output, or manually escape the query value with html.EscapeString() before embedding it in HTML.
